item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted.
Surprisingly low score for such a good skip.

My biggest concern with this isn't anything to do with what it does, but why the Foundation care so much about what happens in the alt. reality. We know what the object does. Record the changing page for record keeping purposes, but why waste the resources trying to find out what's different in the alt. reality?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvote. It's a very basic use of the Alt Dimension historical events trope. The logs are a simple list without direction - every log could be replaced and have no impact on the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This. Came here to see who else caught that. If they knew they were gonna die either way they'd never cooperate at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very good, but I do have a few questions, So, do the D-class' know about the monthly termination? If so, why? How can a news-broadcast explain that a nuclear holocaust has occurred? And, aren't all the pages of the book, except the last one, absolutely meaningless? With that said, Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it would've been more interesting if you had focused on events farther back in the past ("A New Way of Hearing Building" being about Hadrian's Wall in a world where Rome survives?), and more dramatically different worldlines. Still, very fascinating SCP. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know, “there is no canon,” but this violates pretty well-established tenets of the unwritten canon:

If at any time, the last page of SCP-1399 is observed to change, three D-class personnel are to be recruited on a voluntary basis; due to their subsequent removal from monthly terminations, volunteers have proved relatively easy to find. To prevent complications, personnel are to be briefed of the effects of SCP-1399 before exposure.

D-Class personnel most definitely do not know about monthly termination. They've generally been promised things like parole or other such bull. So how would you get volunteers by promising not to kill them?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh, a hurricane was bound to hit the east coast eventually. At least it was only a Category One.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So uh…in light of recent events, that last entry in Document 1399B gave me a bit of a chill.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I very much enjoy this idea.

Especially when one of those foundations fails, and the next message received is a gutteral, inhuman "thank you" note.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a good example of the cliche list coming in handy:

17. Things what just tell you random information that be's all mysterious liek.

OK, so we know where it's coming from, but we're still just learning random information about how assorted events could have gone wrong—sometimes bizzarely, sometimes reasonably. I've seen enough "message from an alternate reality woooo!", or really just "alternate reality woooo!" that it's not interesting anymore.

What's supposed to make this stand out? There's plenty of things that mess with your senses, and this one doesn't strike me as novel. It's just not gripping. It's not a bad idea, it's not poorly written, but there's nothing that makes me think it's anything special.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Simple answer, subjects hear news reports or people discussing recent events. I'm pretty sure that any of those would be the only topic of conversation for quite some time.

More elaborate (unsupported) answer, alternate time-lines have alternate Foundations. Foundation B would have their own versions of 1399 subjects, reporting on sounds they were hearing from Foundation C, and so on. The 1399 effect could be used as a form of one-way radio. Whenever a world-shaking disaster happens, that Foundation puts a recorded explanation with all relevant detials on a loop in the most recent subjects chamber. He doesn't register hearing it, but his counterpart in whatever universe is tuning in on that one would hear and relay the information to that version of the Foundation. The end goal would be to increase the odds that the Foundation was able to maintain itself, or even prevent these disasters in at least one universe. Of course, none of this is in the article, it's just my random thinking on what could be done with this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, the major spread of problems from a meltdown are steam and smoke, along with any other particulate (or larger) bits of debris sent up from the exposed core.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Too conflicted to vote - I liked the basic idea and wanted to upvote, until I read the log. I am trying to see how you could possibly explain deducing those changes and I can't find a way. So either you need to really wow me with an explanation or you need to change that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is there any good reason you feel compelled to not place the containment procedures inside of the containment procedures section? Also, your rewrite that added the procedure in another section turned my holdoff into a downvote. Unless you can explain to me why, I'm too irritated to upvote by this break in standard formatting.

I do like the idea though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have now elaborated, thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Bulked.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Stylistic decision: including the testing procedure in the containment protocols messes with the arc of the SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for the commentary and thank you for the upvote. I had some other ideas on what the book would do, but this one worked best for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sure the way the alternate history attaches here is good - for one ,seems like a free ride to make it more compelling, and .. not quite what I'd imagine under the premise. Something both.. funkier than sound from parallel dimension, but more related to the object it's "another way of hearing" of. (Also .by the time I read and voted, the plot holes have been fixed.)
That said.
There's something about the object itself that super works for me. A book filled with things like "Another way of hearing trains" that causes auditory perception to change.. i just friggin' love the imagery you set up there enough that it primed me enough to consider the rest of the article passable
And that's worth an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Changed it to radioactive steam, which seems to be a possible result of a nuclear meltdown form my research.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed RAS syndrome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alright this concept is unique. It's also pretty interesting, not my favorite but that's a personal gripe. I think the best thing to do with the containment procedures, is expand on the "outlined procedures". I'm glad you at least TOUCHED on what to do if it changes, but something to elaborate is kind of needed.

Also something like "a standard safe object storage unit" fits better in the beginning. But that's just me possibly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Needs work on the containment procedures. Would upvote if that section was bulked out a little, no vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If the page is observed to change in any way, follow outlined procedures.

Interesting SCP but this bit seems very wrong. Isn't the point of the Containment procedures section to give us the outlined procedures? I really think they should be included here not scattered later in the document.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Edit: never mind

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can see where you wanted to go with it, but it doesn't pan out right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted but yeah, fix that bit about the earthquakes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I'm cutting the comment about preferring blind D-class from Drew's comments: It was kinda stupid to begin with. Thanks for the feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not gonna lie, I completely forgot that if they were blind, they wouldn't be able to read messages written to them. Thanks for catching it: I'll fix that.
As for the Fukishima Daiichi one, I wasn't quite sure about the science on it: thanks for the input, I'll fix that one up too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the advice! I'm probably going to add interviews with D-class personell to clarify the establishing method. As for the blank pages, I thought it would be more interesting if it was a more limited dynamic: unless the Foundation keeps careful track, they'll miss a universe.
What do you think I should add for the containment procedures?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm thirding this point. Even though other people have said it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just my bit on the subject of blind class Ds.

They would only work if they're both very cooperative, and well informed of what they're about to undergo. Telling them that they'll be hearing another reality from now on kind of removes the cooperative element. And blind people would be MORE disoriented because of the lack of any stimuli besides feeling. Take it from someone who's actually got a major vision impairment, your hearing is important to you when your seeing isn't so much.

And my derpy self hit the wrong button. Meh, sort of fits with Drew's comment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This phenomenon has been noted to create considerable distress among D-class personnel due to disorientation from a difference in auditory and visual stimuli: for this reason it is advised to use blind D-class personnel when possible

Nnngh. This is veeeeery borderline for me. It makes sense at first glance, but how do we communicate with them at that point? They only hear things from the alternate history, so vocal communication is right out. I guess we could use Braille interactions, but that seems… inefficient. Why not just use sighted people and give them high-class hearing dampeners, so they aren't disoriented by the difference except when they're being experimented on?

I, too, would like a better explanation for how they're able to map such wide-ranging historical differences. After all, someone just living in a Foundation facility isn't going to randomly hear "oh hey! Commandos killed Prince William and now England is in a civil war!" Even just saying that the D-class is plunked in front of a TV turned to a 24-hour news station would be enough. And as for the audiovisual disconnect there, have them sit with their back to the TV and write down a summary of each story as they hear them.

Finally, I have a problem with the Fukishima Daiichi one. An immediate total meltdown of the plant would've been disastrous, yes (and gods know the existing situation is already bad enough to be considered as bad or worse than Chernobyl), but it wouldn't have caused further earthquakes or tsunamis. And even in this world, the aftershocks from that earthquake were 6.0+, which would have been considered a disaster-level earthquake at any other time or place.

Edit:

a series of resultant aftershocks cause numerous tidal waves across the world. Multiple sub-sea-level nations wiped from existence

Also, no. Earthquakes/aftershocks are localized and even a large sub-sea one wouldn't cause tidal waves around the world. The Indian Ocean tsunami was one of the worst and most wide-spread ones in recorded history, and it didn't wipe nations off the map. Plus, exactly how many sub-sea-level nations are actually close enough to a coastline that a tsunami would affect them? The nations most likely to be affected by tsunamis are, by definition, above sea-level, or they would already be underwater.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice job. This is very unique and interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted, as I'm a sucker for alternate history scenarios. I do agree we definitely need some indication how information about the diversion is being 'mapped' - even if it's just sitting them in front of a TV like Smapti suggests.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed. Elaborating on how we gleaned the info about these worlds would be good. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. The containment procedures are a little thin, and using blind people as test subjects is a bit cliche, but the effect works for me and I like the use of the AU scenarios.

May I suggest elaborating on how we determine the nature of the alternate reality? Since the effect is sound-based, it seems we could sit them in front of a TV tuned to CNN and have them copy down the breaking news from the other reality they're hearing.

EDIT: If I were writing this SCP, i'd say that there a number of blank pages in the back of the book, and that each new point of divergence results in one of the blank pages having a new image and text presented on it, rather than overwriting the last page over and over. But that's just me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It doesn't seem to be much more different then 'what would happen if an alternate reality in which X historical point changed' except multiple of them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Crap crap crap crap crap. This is interesting as all hell but I don't want to upvote because of plot holes (i.e. how do we gather how much this event has changed from hearing the alternate world?)
Quite conflicted. Hesitant downvote; fix a few plot holes and tone issues and I'll rape the upvote button with my mouse pointer

item.title: Hmmm...
item.content:encoded: Interesting and evocative. Upvoted.
